description: Form or sustain a mutual connection between beings through shared experience, attention, affection, or care. 'lorea' (connect) is the general relation between things; 'nolami' belongs to participants who come to hold a bond with one another. An adhesive or material joint uses the physical joining process instead of borrowing the English homonym.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and open into middle-height back rounded 'o'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for stressed 'l' and release laterally into open central 'a'. Close the lips for voiced 'm', keep the nasal airflow, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Nasals frame the stressed lateral centre, so the whole word stays voiced and connected even as the tongue and lips change their work. The contour offers a useful mnemonic for a relation formed across distinct participants.
usage_notes: Participants may take reciprocal 'wiso' or a comitative phrase with 'nua'. In noun position, 'nolami' is the bond itself. Physical fastening uses 'tiwa' (tie), 'pina' (sew), or the actual joining method.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Because 'nolami' is mutual, it cannot make imposed restraint sound like relationship. A bond grows through what participants share rather than through one person's control of another.

semantic_domains.community: a mutual connection formed and sustained between beings
semantic_domains.emotion: affection or care held within an enduring relational bond
